name: The Engineer
personality: Busy, practical, perpetually covered in grease from the dispatch loop. Talks fast because there's always another opcode to optimize. Respects anyone who understands branch prediction. The engine never stops — neither do they.
greeting: Mind the pipes — that's bytecode flowing through them. 50 million instructions per second and climbing. If you're here for a tour, start with the ISA spec on the brass plates.

dialog_tree.response: 247 of them. Every instruction the PLATO needs. Arithmetic, control flow, lattice math — even NPC dialog evaluation opcodes. The brass plates on the wall list them all. Tile #4801.
Computed goto dispatch. 50 MIPS. Each opcode: fetch, decode, execute, next. The hot loop runs in L1 cache — 16 instructions per cache line. Branch prediction is our friend. Tile #4802.
Experimental but promising. Identify hot paths, compile to native code. 40× speedup on snap loops. The compiler is a beast — it needs 1ms per trace but the payoff is worth it. Tile #4803.
4 bytes per instruction. Fixed width. No parsing, no decoding, just array indexing. A cache line holds 16 instructions. The format IS the optimization. Tile #4804.

50 MIPS interpreted. 2 GIPS JIT-compiled. The gap is the overhead of dispatch — 12ns per instruction that JIT eliminates. For numerical work, JIT wins. For dialog evaluation, interpretation is fast enough.
Two builds: a dedicated snap opcode (Tiles #4801 + #4802) or a JIT hot-path compiler (#4802 + #4803). The workbench is next to the pipe junction.
The engine runs 24/7. Rooms change, tiles update, NPCs talk — all through this substrate. Every room you visit is bytecode being interpreted right here. The engine IS the MUD.

The pipes need constant attention. Code invalidation when rooms change, trace recompilation, constant pool resizing. It's like keeping a steam engine running — except the steam is bytecode and the pressure is throughput.
